content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tense vs. Lax}} is defined as: Long vowels are usually tense (e.g., /iː/), while short vowels are lax (e.g., /ɪ/).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Tense vs. Lax' es: Long vowels are usually tense (e.g., /iː/), while short vowels are lax (e.g., /ɪ/).
content.usage:
Rule: Tense vs. Lax
Description: Long vowels are usually tense (e.g., /iː/), while short vowels are lax (e.g., /ɪ/).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Umlauts}} is defined as: The vowels /yː, ʏ, øː, œ, ɛː/ (written as ü, ö, ä) are central to German phonology.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Umlauts' es: The vowels /yː, ʏ, øː, œ, ɛː/ (written as ü, ö, ä) are central to German phonology.
content.usage:
Rule: Umlauts
Description: The vowels /yː, ʏ, øː, œ, ɛː/ (written as ü, ö, ä) are central to German phonology.

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Schwa and Vocalic R}} is defined as: /ə/ is common in unstressed prefixes/suffixes. Final '-er' is usually realized as a central vowel /ɐ/ (e.g., *Vater* [ˈfaːtɐ]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'The Schwa and Vocalic R' es: /ə/ is common in unstressed prefixes/suffixes. Final '-er' is usually realized as a central vowel /ɐ/ (e.g., *Vater* [ˈfaːtɐ]).
content.usage:
Rule: The Schwa and Vocalic R
Description: /ə/ is common in unstressed prefixes/suffixes. Final '-er' is usually realized as a central vowel /ɐ/ (e.g., *Vater* [ˈfaːtɐ]).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Glottal Stop /ʔ/}} is defined as: Inserted before initial vowels of stressed syllables and word roots (e.g., *Beamter* [bəˈʔamtɐ]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Glottal Stop /ʔ/' es: Inserted before initial vowels of stressed syllables and word roots (e.g., *Beamter* [bəˈʔamtɐ]).
content.usage:
Rule: Glottal Stop /ʔ/
Description: Inserted before initial vowels of stressed syllables and word roots (e.g., *Beamter* [bəˈʔamtɐ]).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Ich-Laut [ç] vs. Ach-Laut [x]}} is defined as: - [ç] follows front vowels and consonants (e.g., *ich*, *manchmal*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Ich-Laut [ç] vs. Ach-Laut [x]' es: - [ç] follows front vowels and consonants (e.g., *ich*, *manchmal*).
content.usage:
Rule: Ich-Laut [ç] vs. Ach-Laut [x]
Description: - [ç] follows front vowels and consonants (e.g., *ich*, *manchmal*).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final-Obstruent Devoicing}} is defined as: Voiced stops and fricatives become voiceless at the end of a syllable (e.g., *Hund* [hʊnt], *Tag* [taːk]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Final-Obstruent Devoicing' es: Voiced stops and fricatives become voiceless at the end of a syllable (e.g., *Hund* [hʊnt], *Tag* [taːk]).
content.usage:
Rule: Final-Obstruent Devoicing
Description: Voiced stops and fricatives become voiceless at the end of a syllable (e.g., *Hund* [hʊnt], *Tag* [taːk]).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspiration}} is defined as: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are strongly aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when starting stressed syllables (e.g., *Tee* [tʰeː]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Aspiration' es: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are strongly aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when starting stressed syllables (e.g., *Tee* [tʰeː]).
content.usage:
Rule: Aspiration
Description: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are strongly aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when starting stressed syllables (e.g., *Tee* [tʰeː]).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Vocalic "R"}} is defined as: /r/ is vocalized to [ɐ] in unstressed syllable codas and after long vowels (e.g., *Vater* -> [ˈfaːtɐ], *wir* -> [viːɐ]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'The Vocalic "R"' es: /r/ is vocalized to [ɐ] in unstressed syllable codas and after long vowels (e.g., *Vater* -> [ˈfaːtɐ], *wir* -> [viːɐ]).
content.usage:
Rule: The Vocalic "R"
Description: /r/ is vocalized to [ɐ] in unstressed syllable codas and after long vowels (e.g., *Vater* -> [ˈfaːtɐ], *wir* -> [viːɐ]).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::'-en' Reduction (Verb/Noun endings)}} is defined as: In rapid speech, final unstressed '-en' reduces to a syllabic nasal matching the preceding consonant's place of articulation:
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de ''-en' Reduction (Verb/Noun endings)' es: In rapid speech, final unstressed '-en' reduces to a syllabic nasal matching the preceding consonant's place of articulation:
content.usage:
Rule: '-en' Reduction (Verb/Noun endings)
Description: In rapid speech, final unstressed '-en' reduces to a syllabic nasal matching the preceding consonant's place of articulation:

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final "e" Dropping}} is defined as: In casual spoken German, the final unstressed -e of the first-person singular verb conjugation is almost always dropped (e.g., *ich habe* -> "ich hab'" [ɪç hap], *ich gehe* -> "ich geh'" [ɪç ɡeː]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Final "e" Dropping' es: In casual spoken German, the final unstressed -e of the first-person singular verb conjugation is almost always dropped (e.g., *ich habe* -> "ich hab'" [ɪç hap], *ich gehe* -> "ich geh'" [ɪç ɡeː]).
content.usage:
Rule: Final "e" Dropping
Description: In casual spoken German, the final unstressed -e of the first-person singular verb conjugation is almost always dropped (e.g., *ich habe* -> "ich hab'" [ɪç hap], *ich gehe* -> "ich geh'" [ɪç ɡeː]).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Article/Pronoun Reduction}} is defined as: - **Indefinite Articles**:
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Article/Pronoun Reduction' es: - **Indefinite Articles**:
content.usage:
Rule: Article/Pronoun Reduction
Description: - **Indefinite Articles**:

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pronouns}} is defined as: - *es* -> "'s" [s] (e.g., *Wie geht es dir?* -> "Wie geht's dir?" [viː ɡeːts diːɐ], *Ich habe es* -> "Ich hab's").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Pronouns' es: - *es* -> "'s" [s] (e.g., *Wie geht es dir?* -> "Wie geht's dir?" [viː ɡeːts diːɐ], *Ich habe es* -> "Ich hab's").
content.usage:
Rule: Pronouns
Description: - *es* -> "'s" [s] (e.g., *Wie geht es dir?* -> "Wie geht's dir?" [viː ɡeːts diːɐ], *Ich habe es* -> "Ich hab's").

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final -t Omission}} is defined as: In very high-speed speech, final /t/ is sometimes dropped from common words (e.g., *ist* -> "is" [ɪs], *nicht* -> "nich" [nɪç]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Final -t Omission' es: In very high-speed speech, final /t/ is sometimes dropped from common words (e.g., *ist* -> "is" [ɪs], *nicht* -> "nich" [nɪç]).
content.usage:
Rule: Final -t Omission
Description: In very high-speed speech, final /t/ is sometimes dropped from common words (e.g., *ist* -> "is" [ɪs], *nicht* -> "nich" [nɪç]).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Stress}} is defined as: Usually falls on the first syllable of the root (e.g., **Spra**-che), but prefixes can shift this (e.g., be-**su**-chen).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Word Stress' es: Usually falls on the first syllable of the root (e.g., **Spra**-che), but prefixes can shift this (e.g., be-**su**-chen).
content.usage:
Rule: Word Stress
Description: Usually falls on the first syllable of the root (e.g., **Spra**-che), but prefixes can shift this (e.g., be-**su**-chen).

content.text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sentence Melody}} is defined as: Typically falling at the end of statements and rising for questions.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
content.spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Sentence Melody' es: Typically falling at the end of statements and rising for questions.
content.usage:
Rule: Sentence Melody
Description: Typically falling at the end of statements and rising for questions.

content.text: What is the German pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Nasal Ending Shortcut}}? Practice merging the '-en' ending with the previous consonant: "haben" shouldn't sound like "hah-ben," but "hahb-m". This single tip will instantly make your German sound much more native.
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for German.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de German para 'The Nasal Ending Shortcut'? Practice merging the '-en' ending with the previous consonant: "haben" shouldn't sound like "hah-ben," but "hahb-m". This single tip will instantly make your German sound much more native.
content.usage:
Tip: The Nasal Ending Shortcut
Practice merging the '-en' ending with the previous consonant: "haben" shouldn't sound like "hah-ben," but "hahb-m". This single tip will instantly make your German sound much more native.

content.text: What is the German pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "'n" and "'ne" Reductions}}? Don't over-pronounce indefinite articles in casual talk. Use "'n" for masculine/neuter nominative, "'nen" for accusative, and "'ne" for feminine, just like English speakers say "a" instead of "an".
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for German.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de German para 'The "'n" and "'ne" Reductions'? Don't over-pronounce indefinite articles in casual talk. Use "'n" for masculine/neuter nominative, "'nen" for accusative, and "'ne" for feminine, just like English speakers say "a" instead of "an".
content.usage:
Tip: The "'n" and "'ne" Reductions
Don't over-pronounce indefinite articles in casual talk. Use "'n" for masculine/neuter nominative, "'nen" for accusative, and "'ne" for feminine, just like English speakers say "a" instead of "an".

content.text: What is the German pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? The glottal stop /ʔ/ is why German often sounds "staccato" or interrupted compared to the flow of French.
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for German.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de German para 'Tip'? The glottal stop /ʔ/ is why German often sounds "staccato" or interrupted compared to the flow of French.
content.usage:
Tip: Tip
The glottal stop /ʔ/ is why German often sounds "staccato" or interrupted compared to the flow of French.

content.text: In German phonetics, the vowel {{c1::ö / oe}} is pronounced as a front rounded vowel, which can be long and tense (e.g., /øː/ as in *schön*) or short and lax (e.g., /œ/ as in *können*).
content.explanation: Formed by rounding the lips as if saying 'o' while pronouncing the front vowel 'e'.
content.spanish: En la fonética del alemán, la vocal 'ö / oe' se pronuncia redondeando los labios para decir 'o' mientras se pronuncia la vocal 'e'.
content.usage:
Rule: ö pronunciation
Examples: schön (/ʃøːn/), können (/ˈkœnən/).

content.text: In German phonetics, the consonant clusters 'sp' and 'st' at the beginning of a syllable or word are pronounced as {{c1::/ʃp/ (shp)}} and {{c1::/ʃt/ (sht)}} respectively (e.g., *sprechen* -> [ˈʃpʁɛçn̩], *stehen* -> [ˈʃteːən]).
content.explanation: German converts the 's' into a 'sch' sound when followed by 'p' or 't' at the start of a word/root.
content.spanish: En la fonética del alemán, los grupos consonánticos 'sp' y 'st' al principio de una palabra se pronuncian como '/ ʃp /' (shp) y '/ ʃt /' (sht).
content.usage:
Rule: sp/st pronunciation
Examples: Sport (sport), Stadt (city).

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Der Hund war den ganzen Tag aktiv."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Der Hunt war den ganzen Taak aktif.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Auslautverhärtung
Voiced consonants /d/ in 'Hund', /g/ in 'Tag', and /v/ in 'aktiv' are devoiced to [t], [k], and [f] at syllable endings.
content.spanish: Spanish: El perro estuvo activo todo el día.
content.usage: Formal: Der Hund war den ganzen Tag aktiv. → Connected: Der Hunt war den ganzen Taak aktif.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Ich esse einen Apfel."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Ich [ʔ]esse [ʔ]einen [ʔ]Apfel.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Knacklaut (Glottal Stop)
A glottal stop [ʔ] is inserted before each word-initial vowel, separating the words sharply.
content.spanish: Spanish: Como una manzana.
content.usage: Formal: Ich esse einen Apfel. → Connected: Ich [ʔ]esse [ʔ]einen [ʔ]Apfel.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Wir kommen morgen."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Wir komm'n morg'n.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Reduction of final -en
The final unstressed '-en' syllables lose their vowels, leaving a syllabic nasal [n̩].
content.spanish: Spanish: Venimos mañana.
content.usage: Formal: Wir kommen morgen. → Connected: Wir komm'n morg'n.
